transcript:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-01-08
Start time: 10:30 AM PST
Duration: ~52 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Hayes (Redwood AE), Priya Raman (Redwood SE), Evelyn Chen (Maritime Logic, Head of Search), Marco Diaz (Maritime Logic, Platform Eng), Sofia Ruiz (CTO)

Auto-summary (auto-generated, may be rough):
Redwood ran a hosted API walkthrough focused on embeddings + reranking for Maritime Logic's search relevance needs. Discussed integration options (hosted API vs dedicated), sample latency/cost tradeoffs, Redwood Console observability, and Redwood Optimize recommendations (batching, quantization suggestions). Action items: Redwood to send pricing and run a POC; customer to share dataset and schedule POC kickoff.

Topics covered:
- Quick qualification: current stack, SLAs, traffic profile
- Hosted embeddings endpoint usage and cost/latency expectations
- Reranking endpoint flow: first-pass retrieval, embed/populate vector DB, second-pass rerank via Redwood hosted reranker
- Console demo: token and latency breakdown, per-route chart
- Redwood Optimize suggestions and auto-batching examples
- Security: SOC 2, KMS, VPC/private deployment clarification

Transcript body:
[00:00:00] Jordan Hayes: Hey everyone, thanks for joining, I'm Jordan with Redwood, AE on the account. We have Priya from our SE team. How's everyone doing?
[00:00:08] Evelyn Chen: Doing good, thanks. We've got about 50 minutes, so excited to see the hosted flow.
[00:00:12] Priya Raman: Hi! I will mainly run the demo and answer the nitty-gritty. Quick agenda: we'll do a short qualification, demo the embeddings endpoint and the reranker, show the Console and Optimize tips, then wrap with next steps. Sound good?
[00:00:22] Marco Diaz: Yep, that works. For context: we run a catalog search for maritime equipment—about 12M documents, heavy metadata, hybrid queries, spike traffic during contract renewals. Current infra is Elastic + custom TF-IDF and some ML features. Latency target is sub-150 ms on UI 95th percentile, but we can tolerate 300ms for complex queries.
[00:00:40] Jordan Hayes: Great, that helps. Quick question on privacy/compliance—do you need VPC/on-prem or is hosted okay for the pilot?
[00:00:46] Sofia Ruiz: For initial pilot hosted is fine, but must be SOC 2 compliant and data-at-rest encryption. Later we may need VPC.
[00:00:54] Priya Raman: Totally. Our hosted API is SOC 2, we can support customer-managed KMS for key segregation on Private deployments; for hosted we do envelope encryption and can provide audit logs. I'll drop the SOC 2 and KMS docs after the meeting.
[00:01:05] Evelyn Chen: Perfect. Also interested in how you handle anchors and metadata in embeddings—do you encode metadata into vectors or keep it separate?
[00:01:12] Priya Raman: Good question. Typical pattern: index dense embeddings for text fields and keep structured metadata for filtering in the vector DB. Then the reranker takes the top-K candidates and has access to the full document including metadata to score. We avoid stuffing large structured blobs into the vector.
[00:01:27] [00:01:30] Priya Raman: Ok switching over to the Console screen—bear with me while I share.
[00:01:35] [00:01:40] Priya Raman: So here's a sample Playground call to /embeddings, small payload. You can see token counts and latency on the right; this example uses our medium embedding model which is a good balance for e-commerce catalogs.
[00:01:55] Marco Diaz: That looks similar to our current flow. How do you recommend setting top_k for initial retrieval? We currently do top 100 then rerank.
[00:02:02] Priya Raman: If you have a strong retrieval vector DB we often see top_k in the 50-200 range depending on diversity. For catalogs with highly similar items, starting at 200 and using a reranker reduces false positives. But there are cost implications—embedding + reranker costs scale with candidates. Redwood Optimize suggests a knee-point where incremental reranker cost doesn't improve MRR. We can run a small sweep on your dataset to find that.
[00:02:23] [00:02:30] Jordan Hayes: Quick interjection on price. For hosted pilots we typically recommend a fixed credit pool so you can evaluate quality without large spend surprises; I'll follow up with a pilot pricing sheet.
[00:02:39] Evelyn Chen: Nice. Can you show the reranker API? How much control do we have over features?
[00:02:44] Priya Raman: Absolutely. So the rerank endpoint accepts a list of candidate docs and a query, returns a ranking score and optional explanations. You can pass in additional signals—bm25 scores, recency, business rules—and the reranker model will use them if you include them in the payload. Here's the JSON example in the Console.
[00:03:02] [00:03:15] Priya Raman: Note: the explanation field is not perfect in the transcript, but in the Console you get token-level attention heatmaps for debugging—we call it explainability view.
[00:03:23] Marco Diaz: Do you host the reranker model alongside embeddings in the same cluster? Concerned about cross-region latency for EU users.
[00:03:31] Priya Raman: In hosted mode we support region pinning; the runtime will try to co-locate embedding and rerank traffic in the same region. For stricter SLAs you can go Dedicated or Private. For pilots, region pinning is usually sufficient.
[00:03:45] Sofia Ruiz: Any warm-start for reranker KV cache to reduce cold-start latency for repeated queries?
[00:03:50] Priya Raman: We have KV cache and prefix caching at the runtime; small warmup is common. The Console shows cache hit rates—Optimize will recommend prewarming or batching configs based on your traffic trace.
[00:03:59] [00:04:10] Jordan Hayes: Let's demo a full flow: query -> embeddings -> DB -> rerank. Priya, walk us through the sample we prepped for maritime terms.
[00:04:18] Priya Raman: Sure. I posted a sample request in the chat (link: /playground/session/abc123) — note, placeholder link. Example query: "heavy-lift winch for offshore mooring, 10 ton capacity". First-pass returns 200 vectors; we pass top 200 into /rerank with structured fields: material, dimensions, last_inspection_date. You can see the reranker reprioritizes based on safety certification metadata we fed.
[00:04:45] Evelyn Chen: The ordering change is exactly what we want. But curious about speed: end-to-end for top200 rerank—what's typical?
[00:04:52] Priya Raman: For top200 using our medium reranker model in hosted region, typical p95 is 180-320ms depending on seq lengths and tokens. With batching and smaller top_k you can push p95 under 150ms. We'll show the Console latency breakdown now.
[00:05:12] [00:05:20] Priya Raman: Console: here's token-level breakdown—embedding time, network, reranker compute. You can see the per-route chart and cost per request. There's a toggle for streaming vs non-streaming responses (not relevant to rerank) and per-model cost profiling.
[00:05:35] Marco Diaz: Do you have any built-in metrics for relevance quality so we don't have to build our own eval harness?
[00:05:41] Priya Raman: We provide an evals integration where you upload a labeled set of queries/ground-truth and Redwood returns MRR/Recall/NDCG trends. It also flags regressions when you swap model versions. Our Redwood Optimize will propose model swaps or quantization changes based on that.
[00:05:56] Sofia Ruiz: When you say quantization changes do you mean you can recommend lower precision models for cost savings? Concerned about quality drop.
[00:06:03] Priya Raman: Yes, Optimize can suggest q4/x or int8 variants for rerank or embedder. It simulates tradeoffs and runs sample evals to estimate quality delta. For our customers we put a guardrail: only recommend if quality remains within your threshold. We'll include a sample Optimize report.
[00:06:18] [00:06:25] Evelyn Chen: Any red flags on data retention? We need 30-day retention for logs, but not beyond.
[00:06:30] Jordan Hayes: Hosted has configurable retention windows for logs and audit trails. We can keep access logs for 30 days and longer retention as an add-on. We'll send the retention controls doc.
[00:06:39] Marco Diaz: For the POC, what minimal work do we need to do on our side?
[00:06:43] Priya Raman: Minimal flow: provide ~10k representative docs + 500 labeled queries (or we can annotate if you prefer), an S3 bucket or temporary upload. We'll run an initial sweep to pick top_k, pick model combos, and return a short report with suggested configs. SE will run a short benchmark showing p95 and cost per request.
[00:07:00] [00:07:05] Marco Diaz: Sounds reasonable. How long to get results back?
[00:07:07] Priya Raman: Typical turnaround is 5-7 business days for the initial dataset sweep.
[00:07:12] Evelyn Chen: On instrumentation—can we get per-query explainability for legal audits?
[00:07:17] Priya Raman: The explainability view captures a per-request trace and token-level attention insights that you can export. For strict audits we can enable extended tracing with additional retention—it's an add-on.
[00:07:27] [00:07:40] Jordan Hayes: Quick objection I often hear: "We already have Elastic and it's working." How would you answer that in a pilot?
[00:07:46] Sofia Ruiz: We actually ask that in our exec meetings.
[00:07:48] Priya Raman: We say: we don't expect you to rip-and-replace. Use Redwood to improve recall and ranking on the most valuable queries. The pilot should focus on revenue-impacting pages or tricky intents where Elastic falls short. We can show lift in MRR on that slice and then propose a broader rollout.
[00:08:02] Marco Diaz: Any concerns integrating with our existing vector DB? We use a hosted vector store for scaling now.
[00:08:08] Priya Raman: You can pair Redwood embeddings + your vector DB — just push vectors. Or use our end-to-end hosted pipeline where we manage the vector store. For pilots we often recommend using your existing store to minimize scope. If you do use your store, ensure it supports the metadata filters we expect.
[00:08:23] Evelyn Chen: Do you have SDKs? We prefer Python.
[00:08:26] Jordan Hayes: Yes, Python and JS SDKs with snippet examples for embeddings -> index -> rerank flow. We'll link the quickstart.
[00:08:32] [00:08:40] Priya Raman: Showed code snippet: import redwood; client.embeddings.create(...); then client.rerank.score(...). Small differences: the rerank payload expects candidate array objects with "text", "metadata", and optional "features" like bm25.
[00:08:55] Marco Diaz: We saw in the docs something called "automatic prefix caching" — is that something we enable?
[00:08:59] Priya Raman: It's on by default for hosted to reduce repeated token costs. You can tune cache TTLs in the Console. Optimize will recommend TTLs based on query repetition.
[00:09:12] [00:09:20] Sofia Ruiz: On SLAs—if we move to dedicated capacity what are the throughput guarantees?
[00:09:25] Jordan Hayes: Dedicated comes with committed GPU pools and an SLA tailored to throughput and p95 targets; we'll include the dedicated capacity appendix in the followup materials. For pilots, we recommend hosted then scale to Dedicated once throughput requirements are validated.
[00:09:39] [00:09:50] Evelyn Chen: Any mention of competitors—how do you stack vs Pinecone + bespoke reranker?
[00:09:56] Priya Raman: Competitors like Pinecone are great for vector storage, but Redwood provides the full inference stack and reranking runtime with production optimizations like continuous batching, kernel selection, and built-in evals/observability. That reduces engineering burden and improves unit economics over time.
[00:10:12] Marco Diaz: That clarifies. Ok, last part: logistics — who will be our technical contact for the POC?
[00:10:18] Jordan Hayes: Priya will be SE owner. I'll coordinate pricing and contract. Priya will lead the technical kickoff.
[00:10:24] Priya Raman: Great. We'll do a light onboarding doc before the kickoff that outlines data formats, sample payloads, and expected deliverables.
[00:10:30] [00:10:45] Evelyn Chen: Quick Q: can we try lower-cost quantized embeddings for initial retrieval and a higher-quality reranker for second pass?
[00:10:50] Priya Raman: Absolutely. That's a common pattern: cheap embedder for retrieval + expensive reranker for finals. Optimize can run the sims to show cost/latency/quality tradeoffs.
[00:10:59] Marco Diaz: Noted.
[00:11:05] Jordan Hayes: Any other questions?
[00:11:08] Sofia Ruiz: None from me. This was useful.
[00:11:10] Evelyn Chen: One more thing — do you have examples of business rules in the rerank payload? Like always prefer certified vendors?
[00:11:16] Priya Raman: Yes you can inject hard-scoring rules as features with a high weight or post-process to enforce must-have constraints. We also support a fallback policy in the routing layer if the reranker is not healthy.
[00:11:28] Marco Diaz: Great. Let's recap next steps.
[00:11:30] Jordan Hayes: Recap: Jordan sends pricing/contract; Priya shares rerank tuning guide + eval starter set; Maritime Logic provides sample corpus and queries; schedule SE-led POC kickoff second half of January.
[00:11:46] Evelyn Chen: Perfect. We'll aim to have the data to you by Jan 17.
[00:11:50] Jordan Hayes: Awesome. Thanks everyone, we'll follow up with calendar invites and the docs.
[00:11:55] Priya Raman: Thanks, bye!
[00:11:57] [call ends]

Notes/References shared in chat during call:
- Placeholder: /playground/session/abc123 (sample request)
- Will attach SOC 2 summary, KMS docs, and Redwood Optimize one-pager in follow up

End of transcript.
